exemplar.title: Investigating the effect of magnet stacking

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the effect of magnet stacking

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear presentation of raw and processed data, with labelled axes, units and error bars on graphs
Use of standard deviation to identify outliers and propagation of uncertainties to percentage errors
Initial plots well annotated, facilitating visual assessment of variability
weaknesses: Data tables are cluttered with duplicate columns, missing headers and inconsistent formatting
Unexplained shift from time to speed lacks derivation or calculation details
Instrumental uncertainty justifications are absent and anomalyârejection criteria are vague
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data recording and processing are communicated clearly, and there is meaningful consideration of uncertainties through errorâbar usage and standardâdeviation analysis. However, formatting errors, incomplete derivations (time â speed) and subjective anomaly rejection prevent full precision. This performance sits between the 3 and 4 descriptors, warranting a 4 to reflect clear communication despite notable omissions.

strengths: Research question situated in a very specific and appropriate context linking Faradayâs and Lenzâs laws to electromagnetic braking demonstrations
Comprehensive theoretical grounding and clear identification of variables and their effects
Detailed procedure with labelled photographs and schematic diagrams enabling reproducibility
Thoughtful methodological controls (e.g., dataâlogger coil separation, spirit level, climate control)
weaknesses: Occasional wordiness and implied rather than explicit practical motivation
Omission of key apparatus details (magnet diameter, coil turn count, dataâlogger sampling rate)
Unquantified treatment of confounding variables such as airflow and residual magnetization
Typographical inconsistency in exponent of velocity in freeâbody equation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although a few minor omissions (e.g., exact magnet dimensions, dataâlogger settings) and occasional overâverbosity prevent absolute perfection, the design is otherwise exemplary: the research question is firmly anchored in a precise scientific context, procedural details and controls are thorough, and methodological considerations are fully explained. On balance, the criterion performance meets the highest band descriptors for a 6.

strengths: Conclusion is consistent with the plotted trend and links back to Faradayâs and Lenzâs laws
Relevant scientific context is invoked to interpret the nonâlinear behavior
Qualitative explanation demonstrates awareness of the relationship between eddy currents and fall time
weaknesses: Lacks quantitative reference to trendline gradients or numerical slopes
Logical explanation about net force versus speed is muddled
Use of placeholders and incomplete symbols undermines clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is justified and largely consistent with the analysis, referencing theoretical principles and observed trends. Some logical inconsistencies and absence of numerical support limit execution. Performance aligns with the 3â4 band, meriting a 4 for partly justified conclusions and relevant scientific context comparison.

strengths: Identification of specific methodological weaknesses (magnet alignment, chipping, dataâlogger glitches) and their impacts on precision
Realistic and relevant improvements proposed (marking magnet orientation, higherâgrade magnets, delayed timing start) linked to identified errors
Suggestions demonstrate thoughtful engagement with limitations and path to improved reliability
weaknesses: No quantitative estimation of how each weakness affects uncertainty
Improvements lack numerical evaluation of their expected impact on results
Some suggestions (e.g., exploring different pipe materials) extend beyond refinement into further experimentation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation goes beyond generic statements, describing targeted weaknesses and practical improvements that address the root causes of error. Although the relative impacts are not quantified, the proposals are realistic and wellâjustified. This meets the upper range of the 3â4 band, bordering on explanatory depth required for a 5.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student clearly establishes why magnet stacking in a copper pipe is worth studying, linking it to Faradayâs and Lenzâs laws and to electromagnetic-braking demonstrations.  Relevant theory, everyday demonstrations and specific variables that could influence the outcome (pipe conductivity, residual magnetisation, temperature) are all mentioned, locating the research question in a very specific context rather than a generic one.  Minor weaknesses are that the context is occasionally wordy and the practical motivation is implied more than explicitly stated; nevertheless, the description goes well beyond a broad statement.  Hence the work sits comfortably in the top band but with slight shortcomings that justify the lower mark of the band (5 rather than 6).

Uncertainties in length (Â±0.01 mm) and time (Â±0.005 s) are quoted, propagated to percentage errors and shown as error bars.  Standard deviation is calculated and used for outlier identification.  However, instrumental justification for Â±0.005 s is missing, the same absolute uncertainty is applied to all measurements, and the removal of anomalies is subjective.  Thus the treatment is meaningful but incomplete, matching âevidence with some significant omissionsâ (score 3).

Methodological considerations are identified (tube material, wall thickness, orientation, magnet symmetry, temperature, airflow) and their significance is explained briefly in tables and prose.  Control measures (use of same tube, spirit level, climate-controlled room, inspection of magnets) are sensible and linked to the variables.  While the student explains why each factor matters, quantitative justification (e.g., expected magnitude of temperature effect) is lacking and some controls remain qualitative, so the explanation is not fully developed.  This aligns with the descriptor âdescribedâ trending toward âexplained,â meriting the upper mark of 3-4 (4).

Specific weaknesses are identified (magnet alignment, chipping, data-logger glitches).  The student comments that these factors produce wobble, unreliable timing and systematic errors, hinting at their impact on precision.  The explanation of *relative* impact, however, is brief and qualitativeâthere is no attempt to estimate how much each weakness could alter the results.  This fits the descriptor "describes specific methodological weaknesses" (score 3).

The procedure includes a labelled photograph, schematic diagram, coil separation (50 cm Â± 0.1 cm), drop location and number of trials, allowing another investigator to reproduce the set-up with only a few assumptions.  However, important details such as the exact magnet diameters, individual magnet length increments, wire-turn count of coils, sampling rate of the data logger and criterion for anomaly rejection are omitted or scattered.  Because reproduction is possible but not seamless, the strand best fits âreproduced with few ambiguities,â warranting the top of the 3-4 band (4).

Processing consists of averages, standard deviations, calculation of percentage error and plotting of a smoothed trendline.  These are correct, yet no quantitative model (e.g., terminal-velocity equation or logarithmic fit) is applied and the transition from time to speed lacks derivation.  Data rejection criteria are vague and one datum (0.05 s) is dismissed without recalculating averages.  Consequently, processing is broadly appropriate but with noticeable omissions and inconsistencies, meriting a 3.

Improvementsâmarking magnet orientation, using undamaged high-grade magnets, starting timing after 25 cm fall and exploring different pipe materialsâare realistic and linked to identified errors.  Nevertheless, the explanations of *how much* these changes would improve accuracy remain qualitative and some suggestions (different materials) drift toward extensions rather than refinements.  This situates the work solidly within ârelevant improvements are describedâ at 3.

Raw and processed data are presented, but tables are cluttered, contain formatting errors and occasionally duplicate columns.  Units and significant figures are included for most quantities, and graphs have labelled axes and error bars, yet the shift from time to âspeedâ is unexplained.  Overall the recording/processing is understandable (clear) though not especially precise, fitting the middle band; a score of 3 reflects the mixed quality.

Accepted scientific context (eddây currents, conservation of energy, Faradayâs law) is invoked to interpret the non-linear behaviour, showing some relevant comparison.  However, the discussion remains largely qualitative; no literature values or quantitative predictions are compared, and an apparent contradiction in force analysis is left unresolved.  Hence the comparison is present but not yet fully justified, warranting a mid-band score of 3.

The student states that results contradict the original hypothesis and qualitatively explains how larger stacks lead to shorter times.  The conclusion references the plotted trend and links to Lenzâs and Faradayâs laws, demonstrating some consistency with the analysis.  Yet no quantitative reference to gradients or numerical comparison is provided, and part of the reasoning (greater opposing force gives *less* net force but faster fall) is logically muddled.  Therefore the strand meets âdescribed but not fully consistent,â scoring 3.

annotations.comment: The introduction provides a strong theoretical grounding in Lenzâs law but the practical motivation and explicit link to everyday electromagnetic braking demonstrations could be made more concise and clearly stated.
Equation 1 is correctly displayed with LaTeX formatting and clearly supports the discussion of induced emf, demonstrating precision in theoretical presentation.
The definitions list lacks context linking each symbol explicitly to magnetâstacking experiments. Consider elaborating how Îµ and ÏB relate to coil measurements in this setup.
The research question is specific and situated clearly within the context of copperâtube eddyâcurrent demonstrations, demonstrating strong contextualization.
The hypothesisâs description of nonâlinear behavior is contradictory and lacks clarity on the turning point; refine the statement to specify expected trend changes with justification.
The freeâbody equation uses an exponent of 6 on velocity (kv^6), which is unusual. Provide a derivation or correct the exponent to match theoretical models (e.g., Stokesâ law or quadratic drag).
Derive or cite the damping term (kâv^n) and specify the value of n and k based on pipe geometry and conductivity. This will improve reproducibility.
Using dataâlogger coils placed 50 cm apart reduces human timing error effectively and strengthens the methodological design.
Materials section omits magnet diameter and coil turn count, which are essential for reproducibility. Add these specifications.
Specify coil windings (number of turns, wire gauge) and magnet dimensions in the materials list to allow another investigator to reconstruct the setup exactly.
The variables table clearly identifies manipulated and measured variables along with apparatus and uncertainties, demonstrating solid planning.
Controlledâvariables table does not address residual pipe magnetization quantitatively; consider measuring background field and documenting control.
Airflow is listed as a confounding variable but no estimation of its magnitude is given. Quantify potential drag effects or monitor air currents.
The limitations section effectively acknowledges small imperfections and dataâlogger glitches, demonstrating awareness of reliability factors.
The procedural steps table omits the dataâloggerâs sampling rate and processing algorithm details. Include these for full reproducibility.
The presentation of raw data in an extensive Excel export is informative but the table is cluttered, with duplicate columns and missing headers, hindering clarity.
The description of exported logger data and initial processing is concise and clearly explains entry/exit time subtraction.
Reorganize the rawâdata table to remove empty columns, label axes consistently, and format significant figures uniformly before detailed analysis.
The semiâsorted data table aggregates trials well but lacks consistent unit notation and significantâfigure justification for exit/entry times.
Include a note on consistent use of three decimal places and rationale for rounding to improve precision communication.
The text describes using standard deviation to identify outliers, demonstrating meaningful consideration of uncertainties.
The processed data table includes Î, percentage errors, and standard deviations, indicating comprehensive quantitative uncertainty analysis.
The criteria for excluding anomalous trials are not defined objectively. State a threshold (e.g., >2 SD or >10 % error) for systematic anomaly removal.
The initial scatter plot with anomalies is well annotated with error bars and labels, facilitating visual analysis of data variability.
The conclusionâs explanation that greater opposing force yields faster fall requires clearer logic; it seems to conflate net force reduction with increased speed.
In the conclusion, reference the fitted trendline gradient or numerical slope to quantitatively support the stated relationship.
Graph 2 excluding anomalies shows small error bars and a clear trend, illustrating the improved precision after outlier removal.
The bulletâpoint explanation contains placeholders and incomplete symbols, which undermines clarity. Replace blanks with full descriptions of each process step.
Quantify the impact of identified systematic errors (e.g., misalignment) by estimating how they affect timing uncertainty to strengthen evaluation.
Prevention methods such as marking magnet orientation and using N52 magnets are realistic and wellâjustified, effectively addressing specific error sources.
Estimate quantitatively how marking the magnet orientation reduces alignment error (e.g., by reducing variance from Â±10 ms to Â±5 ms).

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA

similarExemplars.title: Investigating the relationship between induced voltage and the rate of change of magnetic field through a solenoid.

userSubmission.researchQuestion: Investigating the relationship between induced voltage and the rate of change of magnetic field through a solenoid.

similarExemplars.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.

The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.
